_purpose: Material life profile for antebellum plantation, 1820-1860. Covers both enslaved and slaveholding family perspectives. The enslaved perspective appears first in every section and receives equal or greater detail. Material deprivation described here was imposed by slaveholders on enslaved people — it was not a natural condition.
region: Deep South — Cotton Belt (Alabama, Mississippi, Louisiana, Georgia, South Carolina)
population: Enslaved African Americans (majority population on plantations) and slaveholding white families. Note: most white Southerners did NOT own enslaved people — this profile covers the plantation specifically. On a typical cotton plantation of 500+ acres, enslaved people outnumbered the slaveholding family by ratios of 10:1 or higher.

position_guidance.enslaved: The enslaved person had no economic position in the legal sense — they were classified as property. Do NOT use economic framing that implies choice or agency in their material conditions. The material deprivation described in the enslaved sections of this file was IMPOSED, not chosen. Use the Record Silences template for interpretive framing. Economic analysis of enslaved people's lives requires specialized handling — see the editorial_note in the enslaved_ancestor_gap template.
position_guidance.small_slaveholder: Adjust framing significantly. A slaveholder with 1-5 enslaved people lived materially closer to the yeoman farmer than to the planter described here. No Greek Revival house, no imported china, no columned portico. The small slaveholder worked alongside the people they enslaved. Use antebellum_yeoman_farm material life for the physical setting, but acknowledge the presence of enslaved labor. Property records and slave schedules document this position.
position_guidance.large_planter: Default framing. The ancestor was a planter with 20+ enslaved people, documented in slave schedules, tax records, and the full apparatus of planter-class record-keeping. This file's physical descriptions apply directly.

narrative_hooks.enslaved: The material conditions {ancestor_name} lived under on the {plantation_name} place were not chosen — they were imposed by the slaveholder who controlled every aspect of daily existence, from the cabin's construction to the weekly ration of cornmeal and salt pork.
narrative_hooks.small_slaveholder: The {year} slave schedule shows {ancestor_name} holding {number} enslaved people — a small slaveholder who worked the land alongside those they held in bondage, living in a farmhouse, not a mansion, and owning no imported china or columned portico.
narrative_hooks.large_planter: The {plantation_name} plantation in {county} was the world the slaveholder built with enslaved labor — {number} people held in bondage, their work visible in every polished surface, every bale of cotton, every column on the portico.

housing.narrative: Slaveholders confined enslaved people to quarters — rows of single-room cabins set apart from the main house, deliberately positioned so the overseer could monitor movement. The cabins were built cheaply and maintained poorly, because the slaveholder's investment was in the people, not their shelter. The planter's own house, by contrast, grew grander across this period, evolving from a functional dogtrot into a columned Greek Revival statement of wealth extracted from enslaved labor.

details.description: Slaveholders housed enslaved families in single-room log or frame cabins, typically 16 by 18 feet, with dirt floors, stick-and-mud chimneys, and no glass windows — only wooden shutters. A single room served for cooking, eating, sleeping, and all domestic life. Cabins were arranged in rows ('the quarters' or 'the street') within sight of the overseer's house. Construction was minimal: unhewn logs with mud chinking, a puncheon door, a roof of hand-split shingles or boards. Slaveholders provided no furniture; enslaved people built or improvised their own.

Slaveholders allocated one cabin to a family, but families of six or eight people slept in a single room. Beds were wooden platforms or pallets of corn shucks or moss on the dirt floor. Some enslaved people built crude bedsteads from scrap lumber. Children often slept on the floor near the fireplace. Blankets were issued by the slaveholder — typically one per person per year, made of coarse wool or 'Negro cloth.' Privacy did not exist.

Because the cabins were cramped and dark, much of enslaved people's domestic life took place outdoors in the swept-earth yards between cabins. Cooking, washing, childcare, socializing, and craft work happened in the yard. The yard was also where enslaved people maintained small gardens (when the slaveholder permitted), kept chickens, and gathered in the evening after the day's forced labor ended.

details.item: Planter house — dogtrot and I-house forms
details.description: Before 1840, most planter houses in the Cotton Belt were functional rather than grand: the dogtrot (two rooms separated by an open breezeway) or the I-house (two stories, one room deep, central hall). These houses were built of hewn logs or frame construction, with detached kitchen buildings (to reduce fire risk and keep heat out of the main house). Separate outbuildings housed the smokehouse, dairy, loom house, and overseer's quarters.

From the 1830s onward, prosperous planters replaced or expanded their houses into Greek Revival mansions with columned porticos, wide central halls, multiple parlors, and second-floor galleries. These houses were statements of wealth and social aspiration — modeled on pattern books and built by enslaved craftsmen whose skill made the architecture possible. The kitchen remained a separate building, staffed by enslaved cooks.

details.item: Planter house — enslaved domestic workers' presence
details.description: The planter house was not a white-only space. Enslaved domestic workers — cooks, maids, nurses, body servants — spent their working hours inside the house and sometimes slept in attic rooms, hallway pallets, or the children's rooms. The proximity was intimate but not equal: enslaved domestics were always on call, always watched, and always subject to the slaveholder's will.

food_and_cooking.narrative: Slaveholders controlled what enslaved people ate by issuing weekly rations — a system designed to provide enough calories for heavy labor at the lowest possible cost. The standard ration was monotonous and nutritionally incomplete, and enslaved people supplemented it through gardens, hunting, and fishing when the slaveholder allowed. At the planter's own table, the same plantation produced abundant and varied meals, prepared by enslaved cooks whose culinary skill is one of the great unacknowledged contributions to American food culture.

details.description: Slaveholders issued enslaved adults a weekly ration of one peck (about 8 quarts) of cornmeal and 3 to 4 pounds of salt pork or bacon. Some slaveholders substituted sweet potatoes, rice, or molasses. Children received half rations. This diet provided calories for field labor but was severely deficient in vitamins, calcium, and fresh protein. Pellagra (niacin deficiency), rickets, and dental disease were endemic in the quarters.

Enslaved people supplemented rations through kitchen gardens (growing greens, peas, sweet potatoes, okra), hunting (possum, raccoon, rabbit, squirrel), fishing, and foraging (wild berries, nuts, greens). Some slaveholders permitted garden plots; others forbade them to maintain dependency. Chickens were sometimes allowed in the quarters. Enslaved people also developed preservation techniques — drying, smoking, and salting — to extend their food supply beyond what the slaveholder issued.

Enslaved families cooked over open fireplaces in their cabins, using an iron pot (sometimes issued by the slaveholder, sometimes shared among several families), a skillet, and a gourd dipper. Cornbread (hoecake, ashcake, pone) was the staple — mixed from meal and water, baked on a hoe blade or in ashes. One-pot meals of greens, peas, and salt pork were common. On some larger plantations, slaveholders required communal cooking in a central kitchen rather than allowing families to cook independently.

The planter family ate abundantly: breakfast of ham, eggs, biscuits, and coffee; dinner (midday) of roasted or boiled meats (pork, chicken, beef, game), rice, vegetables, cornbread, and preserves; supper of lighter fare. Seasonal fruits, nuts, and dairy products supplemented the diet. All meals were prepared by enslaved cooks in the detached kitchen building and carried to the main house by enslaved servants.

Enslaved cooks were among the most skilled workers on the plantation and among the most valuable in the slaveholder's accounting. They developed the cuisine now recognized as 'Southern cooking' — combining West African techniques and ingredients (okra, black-eyed peas, rice cooking methods, deep frying) with European and Native American foodways. An enslaved cook's knowledge was passed through oral tradition, mother to daughter, and represented a culinary tradition of extraordinary sophistication developed under conditions of bondage.

clothing.narrative: Slaveholders issued enslaved people two sets of clothing per year — summer and winter — made from the cheapest available fabrics. Clothing was a tool of control: it marked enslaved people visually, restricted their ability to pass as free, and reminded them of their dependency. The planter family, by contrast, dressed according to the fashions of their class, wearing imported fabrics and seasonal wardrobes that displayed the wealth that enslaved labor produced.

details.description: Slaveholders distributed clothing twice yearly. The summer issue was typically two shirts and two pairs of trousers (men) or two dresses (women), made from osnaburg — a coarse, undyed cotton-linen blend known as 'Negro cloth.' The winter issue added a wool jacket or coat and a wool hat. Children received a single long shirt until age 10 or 12, regardless of gender. Fabric was rough, poorly sewn, and ill-fitting. Enslaved people altered, mended, and personalized their clothing whenever possible.

Slaveholders issued shoes only for winter — one pair of brogans (heavy, stiff cowhide shoes) per year, often poorly sized. Enslaved people went barefoot from spring through fall, regardless of terrain or labor. The brogans were made cheaply, sometimes on the plantation by enslaved cobblers, and frequently caused blisters and foot injuries. Shoes that wore out before the next annual issue were not replaced.

details.item: Enslaved clothing — personal expression and resistance
details.description: Despite the slaveholder's intent to impose uniformity, enslaved people used clothing for personal expression and cultural identity. Women dyed fabric with plant dyes (indigo, walnut hulls, pokeberry), made head wraps from scraps, and sewed quilts and decorative items. Sunday clothing — often assembled from traded, gifted, or self-made items — was visibly distinct from work clothing. Some enslaved people received cast-off clothing from the planter family; others bartered garden produce or labor for fabric.

Planter women wore cotton, silk, and wool dresses in current fashions, ordered from New Orleans, Charleston, or Northern merchants. Seasonal wardrobes included light cotton and muslin for summer, heavier silks and woolens for winter, and formal attire for balls and church. Planter men wore broadcloth suits, linen shirts, and boots. Children were dressed in miniature adult fashion. All planter clothing was sewn by enslaved seamstresses (for everyday wear) or ordered from urban dressmakers.

details.item: Planter family — textile production on the plantation
details.description: Many plantations maintained a loom house where enslaved women spun thread and wove cloth — primarily the osnaburg and linsey-woolsey used for enslaved people's clothing, but also household textiles (towels, sheets, blankets). The loom house was a specialized workplace staffed by enslaved women whose weaving skill saved the slaveholder the cost of purchased cloth.

hygiene_and_health.narrative: Slaveholders treated enslaved people's health as a property-maintenance issue — they called doctors when an enslaved person's illness threatened the slaveholder's investment, not out of concern for the person's wellbeing. Enslaved people developed their own medical knowledge, drawing on West African herbal traditions and accumulated experience, and practiced healing within the quarters despite the slaveholder's attempts to control all aspects of their lives. Both populations shared the same disease environment — malaria, yellow fever, dysentery, typhoid — but enslaved people suffered disproportionately because of imposed malnutrition, overwork, and crowded living conditions.

details.description: Enslaved people suffered from diseases of poverty imposed by their conditions: respiratory infections (from unventilated cabins), intestinal parasites (from dirt floors and contaminated water), pellagra and nutritional deficiencies (from the ration diet), and dental disease. Infant mortality among enslaved people was roughly double that of whites. Malaria was endemic in the Cotton Belt, and enslaved people who worked in swampy bottomlands during mosquito season were disproportionately affected. Cholera, dysentery, and typhoid epidemics swept the quarters periodically.

Slaveholders called physicians for enslaved people only when the illness was severe enough to threaten a valuable worker's productivity — or life. The doctor's bill was a business expense. Common 'treatments' included bleeding, purging, and doses of calomel (mercury chloride), which often caused more harm than the illness. Slaveholders also purchased patent medicines in bulk for the quarters. Enslaved women who served as plantation nurses provided daily care — dosing, bandaging, and tending the sick — under the slaveholder's direction.

Enslaved people maintained healing traditions rooted in West African herbal knowledge and adapted to American plants. Root doctors and herb women in the quarters knew the properties of sassafras, boneset, mullein, cherry bark, and dozens of other plants. These healers treated illness, attended births (enslaved midwives delivered most babies in the quarters), and provided spiritual healing that combined African cosmology with Christianity. Slaveholders sometimes tolerated and sometimes punished this autonomous medical practice.

details.item: Enslaved health — childbirth and infant mortality
details.description: Enslaved women gave birth in their cabins, attended by enslaved midwives. Slaveholders rarely called physicians for childbirth unless complications arose. Women were expected to return to field labor within days or weeks of giving birth. Infant mortality was catastrophic: roughly 50% of enslaved children died before age five, due to maternal malnutrition, unsanitary conditions, tetanus (from cutting the umbilical cord with unsterilized tools), and sudden infant death related to the exhaustion of overworked mothers.

Planter families had better access to physicians, but antebellum medicine offered little advantage. Planters and their families died of the same diseases — malaria, yellow fever, typhoid — that killed enslaved people, though at lower rates because of better nutrition, less physical exhaustion, and less crowded housing. Planter women died in childbirth at significant rates. Wealthy planters sent family members to mineral springs or Northern cities for health, an option unavailable to enslaved people.

Enslaved quarters typically shared a single well or spring, which was also used for washing. Privies (when they existed) were communal and rarely maintained. Slaveholders did not invest in sanitation for the quarters. The planter house had a dedicated well, a separate kitchen garden, and somewhat better sanitation — though by modern standards, conditions were primitive across the plantation. Bathing for enslaved people happened in creeks, ponds, or washtubs, usually on Saturday evenings.

transportation.narrative: For enslaved people, movement was controlled, monitored, and punished. The pass system required enslaved people to carry written permission from the slaveholder to travel beyond the plantation — and slave patrols enforced this system with violence. Slaveholders, by contrast, moved freely by carriage, horseback, and steamboat, traveling to town, to neighboring plantations, and to cities for business and pleasure. The contrast between enslaved immobility and slaveholder mobility was one of the sharpest material expressions of the power relationship.

details.description: Enslaved people who left the plantation without a written pass from the slaveholder were subject to arrest, whipping, or worse by slave patrols ('patterollers') — groups of white men who rode the roads at night specifically to intercept enslaved people without passes. The pass system restricted enslaved people's movement to the plantation, the immediate surrounding area, and specific authorized errands. Even with a pass, an enslaved person could be stopped, questioned, and harassed by any white person.

Enslaved people traveled on foot. They walked to the fields, walked between plantations when the slaveholder permitted visits, walked to church (when allowed to attend), and walked to the nearest town on rare authorized trips. Shoes were issued only for winter; most travel was barefoot. Distances of 5 to 15 miles were routine for visits between neighboring plantations. Running away — the ultimate unauthorized movement — required navigating by foot through swamps, forests, and unfamiliar territory, often at night.

details.item: Enslaved movement — forced transportation in the domestic slave trade
details.description: The most devastating form of transportation enslaved people experienced was forced sale and removal through the domestic slave trade. Enslaved people were marched in coffles (chained groups) from the Upper South to the Cotton Belt, transported by ship from the Chesapeake to New Orleans, or sold at auction and carried to a new plantation by wagon. This forced migration separated families permanently and moved roughly one million enslaved people into the Deep South between 1790 and 1860.

Planters traveled by horseback for daily plantation business, by carriage or buggy for trips to town and social visits, and by steamboat for longer journeys to New Orleans, Mobile, or Natchez. The planter's carriage was a status symbol — maintained and driven by enslaved coachmen. Horseback riding was a marker of planter masculinity. Planter women traveled by carriage, accompanied by enslaved attendants.

details.item: Planter transportation — steamboat and river travel
details.description: Steamboats on the Mississippi, Alabama, and Savannah rivers connected plantation districts to commercial cities. Planters shipped cotton to market by steamboat and traveled to New Orleans, Mobile, and Charleston for business, shopping, and social seasons. Steamboat travel was a planter-class experience — enslaved people were transported on steamboats as cargo (in the slave trade) or as attendants to their slaveholders, not as independent travelers.

communication.narrative: Slaveholders used literacy as a tool of control — most Southern states made it illegal to teach enslaved people to read or write, precisely because literacy threatened the slaveholder's monopoly on information. Enslaved people developed alternative communication systems: oral tradition, coded songs, secret messages, and kinship networks that carried information across plantation boundaries. The planter class, meanwhile, was deeply literate and connected — by mail, newspapers, and political networks — to the wider world whose benefits they monopolized.

details.description: By the 1830s, every Deep South state had laws prohibiting the teaching of enslaved people to read or write. These laws were enforced with whippings, fines, and imprisonment — for both the teacher and the learner. Despite these laws, an estimated 5-10% of enslaved people achieved some degree of literacy, learning in secret from sympathetic whites, from enslaved people who already knew, or by self-teaching with stolen books and newspapers. Literacy was dangerous knowledge — and enslaved people who possessed it guarded and shared it carefully.

details.item: Enslaved communication — oral tradition and memory
details.description: In the absence of written records, enslaved people maintained family histories, genealogies, medical knowledge, agricultural skills, and cultural traditions through oral transmission. Elders told stories, sang songs, and recited genealogies that preserved knowledge across generations. This oral tradition was not a substitute for literacy — it was a parallel system of knowledge that carried information literacy could not: tone, emotion, spiritual meaning, and the unwritten history of the community.

details.item: Enslaved communication — coded messages in song and craft
details.description: Enslaved people embedded messages in spirituals, work songs, and (according to some scholars) quilt patterns. Songs like 'Follow the Drinking Gourd' and 'Steal Away' carried double meanings — religious lyrics on the surface, navigational or organizational information beneath. The extent and specificity of these coded systems is debated by historians, but the existence of covert communication channels is well documented. Drum communication, practiced in Africa, was prohibited by slaveholders who feared its organizing potential.

details.item: Enslaved communication — kinship networks across plantations
details.description: Enslaved people maintained communication networks that crossed plantation boundaries through 'abroad marriages' (marriages between people on different plantations), church gatherings, hiring-out arrangements, and authorized or unauthorized visits. News of slave sales, runaways, punishment, and opportunities traveled through these networks with remarkable speed. The domestic slave trade disrupted but did not destroy these networks — sold family members sometimes maintained contact across hundreds of miles through oral messages carried by travelers.

details.item: Planter communication — mail, newspapers, and politics
details.description: Planter families communicated by mail (delivered by enslaved riders or through the U.S. postal system), read newspapers (weekly county papers and, in larger towns, daily papers from New Orleans, Charleston, or Mobile), and participated in political networks through elections, militia service, and court days. Planters corresponded with cotton factors in port cities, exchanged agricultural advice, and maintained social connections through letters. The planter's library — when one existed — typically included law books, agricultural journals, the Bible, and popular literature.

entertainment_and_social_life.narrative: Enslaved people carved out spaces for joy, celebration, and cultural expression despite the slaveholder's control over their time and bodies. Music, storytelling, and religious worship — often conducted in secret to avoid the slaveholder's interference — sustained the community's spirit and preserved African cultural traditions. The planter class maintained an elaborate social life built on leisure that enslaved labor made possible: balls, barbecues, horse races, and the church-centered social calendar of the Southern gentry.

details.item: Enslaved social life — music and dance
details.description: Enslaved people created and sustained musical traditions of extraordinary power — field hollers, work songs, spirituals, ring shouts, and secular songs played on fiddles, banjos (an instrument of African origin), drums (where permitted), and improvised percussion. Saturday night dances in the quarters provided release from the week's labor. Slaveholders sometimes required enslaved musicians to perform at planter social events — an appropriation of talent that enslaved people sometimes turned to their own advantage, earning tips or favors.

Enslaved people worshipped both in slaveholder-supervised services (where white ministers preached obedience) and in secret gatherings — 'hush harbors' or 'brush arbors' — where they practiced a Christianity fused with African spiritual traditions. These secret services featured call-and-response preaching, ecstatic worship, the ring shout, and spirituals that spoke of deliverance from bondage. Religious gatherings were the most autonomous social space enslaved people possessed, and slaveholders correctly recognized them as potential sites of resistance.

details.item: Enslaved social life — storytelling and oral performance
details.description: Evening storytelling in the quarters was entertainment, education, and cultural preservation. Trickster tales (Brer Rabbit and similar stories rooted in West African traditions) taught survival strategies: the weak outsmarting the strong through cleverness. Ghost stories, moral tales, and accounts of the African past circulated through the quarters. Storytelling was a communal act — performed for an audience that responded, challenged, and elaborated.

details.item: Enslaved social life — holidays and permitted celebrations
details.description: Christmas was typically the only extended break from forced labor — slaveholders granted 3 to 6 days off, sometimes distributed small gifts (tobacco, fabric, extra food), and permitted visiting between plantations. Corn shuckings were communal work events that slaveholders organized as celebrations, with food, drink, and music provided to encourage fast work. Enslaved people used these occasions for socializing, courtship, and family reunions, investing slaveholder-granted holidays with their own cultural meaning.

details.item: Planter social life — balls, barbecues, and visiting
details.description: Planter social life revolved around visiting (extended stays at neighboring plantations), balls and dances (held in plantation houses or town halls), barbecues and fish fries, and horse racing. These events reinforced class solidarity, arranged marriages, and displayed wealth. Planter women organized and managed social events while enslaved servants performed all the labor — cooking, serving, cleaning, and attending guests.

details.item: Planter social life — church as social center
details.description: The planter church (Baptist, Methodist, Presbyterian, or Episcopal, depending on the region) was the primary social institution for white families. Sunday services were followed by socializing on the church grounds — exchanging news, conducting informal business, and maintaining social ties. Church governance (deacons, vestrymen, trustees) was dominated by planters and reinforced their social authority. Camp meetings and revivals provided multiday social events.

household_goods.narrative: The material possessions in an enslaved person's cabin were minimal — not because enslaved people did not desire comfort or beauty, but because slaveholders provided almost nothing and enslaved people had severely limited means to acquire goods independently. What possessions existed in the quarters — a quilt, a carved spoon, a clay pot — were often handmade and carried cultural significance beyond their practical function. The planter house, by contrast, displayed imported furniture, china, and silver that advertised the family's wealth and gentility, all maintained by enslaved domestic workers who handled these objects daily but owned nothing.

details.item: Enslaved quarters — cooking and eating implements
details.description: A typical enslaved family's household goods included an iron pot (often shared between families), a skillet, a gourd dipper, wooden or gourd bowls, and possibly a few tin cups or plates. Spoons were carved from wood. Knives were scarce — slaveholders restricted access to sharp tools. Water was carried from the well in buckets or gourds. These minimal implements served all the family's cooking and eating needs.

Bedding consisted of a pallet (a cloth sack stuffed with corn shucks, Spanish moss, or cotton) laid on the floor or on a rough wooden platform. Slaveholders issued one blanket per person per year. Enslaved women made quilts from scraps of worn-out clothing and fabric remnants — quilts that were both practical necessities and works of art embodying African American aesthetic traditions. A handmade quilt was often the most valued possession in the cabin.

details.item: Enslaved quarters — personal and cultural objects
details.description: Despite extreme material deprivation, enslaved people created and maintained personal possessions: carved wooden figures, clay pipes, beads, buttons repurposed as ornaments, handmade baskets (using African coiling techniques), and small containers for personal items. Archaeological excavations of slave quarters have uncovered coins, glass beads, shells, and other small objects that suggest both personal adornment and spiritual practice. These objects represent human dignity asserted against a system designed to deny it.

The planter parlor displayed the family's wealth and taste: mahogany or rosewood furniture (sofas, chairs, center tables, sideboards) purchased from New Orleans, Charleston, or Northern cities; upholstered seating with horsehair or damask fabric; oil portraits and framed prints on the walls; curtains and carpets imported from Europe. A piano or melodeon signified refined femininity. Parlor furnishings were often the most expensive movable property listed in estate inventories — sometimes valued higher than the enslaved people who dusted them.

details.item: Planter house — dining and kitchen goods
details.description: Planter dining tables were set with imported china (English transferware was standard; French porcelain for the wealthiest), coin silver or plated flatware, cut glass, and linen tablecloths. A full dinner service for 12 or more was standard for a prosperous planter. The detached kitchen building was equipped with iron cookware, Dutch ovens, andirons, trammels, and roasting spits — all operated by enslaved cooks who possessed the skill but not the ownership of these tools.

details.item: Planter house — timekeeping and luxury items
details.description: A tall-case clock (grandfather clock) or mantel clock was a prized possession that signified the planter's mastery of time — a bitter irony on a plantation where enslaved people's time was entirely controlled by others. Other luxury goods included books, maps, mirrors, candelabra, and writing desks. Enslaved people in the quarters told time by the sun, the rooster, and the overseer's horn; the clock in the big house kept time for the slaveholder's convenience, not theirs.

sensory_snapshot: Dawn mist hangs low over white cotton rows that stretch to the treeline, bolls splitting open. Behind the big house — columns bone-white, shutters dark green — a detached kitchen exhales woodsmoke carrying the smell of salt pork and corn pone. Down the dirt lane, past the smokehouse and gin house, the quarters begin: a double row of weathered cabins with stick chimneys, swept-earth yards, iron pots on tripods over ash-gray fire circles. The smell of the place is layered — cotton dust, turned earth, mule-lot manure, curing meat from the smokehouse, and under everything the heavy rot-and-bloom scent of bottomland. Mockingbirds call from live oaks draped in Spanish moss. The overseer's horn has not yet sounded. Chimney smoke rises and a hound stretches in the lane.
narrative_hooks: The cabin where {ancestor_name} lived on the {plantation_name} place in {county} was sixteen feet by eighteen — one room for a family of {number}. The floor was packed dirt. The chimney was sticks and mud. The door was a single plank. The slaveholder who owned the big house with its columned porch and imported piano also owned {ancestor_name}, and the difference between their dwellings measured the distance between those two facts.

Every Saturday evening, the slaveholder's overseer distributed the week's rations to each family in the quarters: a peck of cornmeal, three pounds of salt pork. That was what {ancestor_name}'s family was given to eat for seven days of forced labor in the cotton fields. What {ancestor_name}'s family actually ate — the greens from the garden, the fish from the creek, the possum caught at night — was what they provided for themselves, in the hours the slaveholder did not claim.

Twice a year the slaveholder issued clothing to the quarters — two suits of osnaburg in spring, a wool jacket and brogans in fall. {Ancestor_name} wore what the slaveholder provided because there was no choice. But on Sundays, in the clothes they had dyed, altered, and assembled themselves, the people in the quarters looked like themselves rather than like the slaveholder's property.

When {ancestor_name} was born in the cabin in the quarters, an enslaved midwife — not a doctor — attended the birth. The slaveholder would call a physician only if the mother's life was in danger, because a dead enslaved woman was a financial loss. The midwife's knowledge, passed down from her own mother and grandmother, drew on traditions that stretched back to West Africa and forward through generations of women who healed under a system that treated their bodies as investments.
source_references: Vlach, John Michael. 'Back of the Big House: The Architecture of Plantation Slavery.' University of North Carolina Press, 1993.
Covey, Herbert C. and Dwight Eisnach. 'What the Slaves Ate: Recollections of African American Foods and Foodways from the Slave Narratives.' Greenwood Press, 2009.
Savitt, Todd L. 'Medicine and Slavery: The Diseases and Health Care of Blacks in Antebellum Virginia.' University of Illinois Press, 1978.
Camp, Stephanie M.H. 'Closer to Freedom: Enslaved Women and Everyday Resistance in the Plantation South.' University of North Carolina Press, 2004.
Fox-Genovese, Elizabeth. 'Within the Plantation Household: Black and White Women of the Old South.' University of North Carolina Press, 1988.
Baptist, Edward E. 'The Half Has Never Been Told: Slavery and the Making of American Capitalism.' Basic Books, 2014.
Foster, Helen Bradley. 'New Raiments of Self: African American Clothing in the Antebellum South.' Berg Publishers, 1997.
Genovese, Eugene D. 'Roll, Jordan, Roll: The World the Slaves Made.' Pantheon Books, 1974.
Berlin, Ira. 'Many Thousands Gone: The First Two Centuries of Slavery in North America.' Harvard University Press, 1998.
Johnson, Walter. 'Soul by Soul: Life Inside the Antebellum Slave Market.' Harvard University Press, 1999.
WPA Federal Writers' Project. 'Slave Narratives: A Folk History of Slavery in the United States.' Library of Congress, 1936-1938. (Primary source — oral histories of formerly enslaved people.)
Singleton, Theresa A. 'The Archaeology of Slavery and Plantation Life.' Academic Press, 1985.
